questions.text: Which of the following are true regarding endoscope selection in gynecologic surgery? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: A) A 0-degree telescope provides the widest field of view in laparoscopy.
B) A 70-degree cystoscope is best for viewing the anterior and lateral bladder walls.
C) A 30-degree laparoscope provides a wider view than a 0-degree laparoscope.
D) Operative hysteroscopy with a 0-degree scope provides a consistent view of instruments.
E) A 30-degree hysteroscope allows visualization of cornual recesses with simple rotation.

questions.explanation: A 0° laparoscope has a narrower field than 30°. A 70° cystoscope optimizes anterior/lateral bladder views. Operative hysteroscopy typically uses a 0° lens for constant instrument visualization; a 30° diagnostic scope easily visualizes cornual recesses by rotation.

questions.text: Which of the following factors contribute to increased surgical fire risk? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: A) Use of alcohol-based skin prep not allowed to fully dry
B) Use of a bipolar cautery device with glycine distension
C) Open delivery of oxygen (e.g., nasal cannula)
D) Use of monopolar electrosurgery
E) CO₂ laparoscopic environment

questions.explanation: Fire risk rises with alcohol prep (if not dry), open oxygen, and ignition sources (monopolar). Glycine/bipolar are fluid/energy compatibility issues, not fire risks. CO₂ is nonflammable.

questions.text: Which of the following are true regarding visualization during laparoscopic surgery? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: A) Fogging can be prevented by pre-warming the laparoscope.
B) Smoke from electrosurgery can cause complete loss of video signal.
C) Intraoperative blood can impair visibility.
D) Disconnection of the light source may result in a black screen.
E) Fogging is best treated by increasing intra-abdominal pressure.

questions.explanation: Warm the scope or use antifog to prevent fogging. Blood degrades the view. Light-source/camera disconnection can black out the screen. Smoke reduces visibility but doesn’t eliminate the image; pressure doesn’t fix fogging.

questions.text: Which of the following techniques can improve visualization during hysteroscopy? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: A) Increase fluid outflow and apply suction.
B) Remove the hysteroscope and perform blind sharp curettage as a primary strategy
C) Temporarily increase intrauterine pressure to 80–100 mmHg.
D) Discontinue the procedure if blood is present.
E) Schedule the procedure during the early proliferative phase.

questions.explanation: Suction/outflow clears blood. Briefly increasing pressure can help; early proliferative timing minimizes bleeding. Blind curettage and stopping as next steps are not preferred.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are incorrect regarding poor uterine distension during hysteroscopy? (Select all that are incorrect.)

answerOptions.text: A) Routine mechanical dilation is always recommended prior to hysteroscopy.
B) An overdilated cervix may result in poor distension.
C) Upsizing the hysteroscope or clamping the cervix can reduce fluid leakage during overdilation.
D) Low intrauterine pressure contributes to under-distension.
E) Purse-string sutures might tighten the cervical seal during overdilation if other measures fail.

questions.explanation: Routine dilation isn’t recommended and may worsen leakage. Overdilation, low pressure, and inadequate sealing reduce distension; upsizing/clamping/purse-string can help.
questions.submodule: Module 3.5 – Managing Poor Uterine Distension During Hysteroscopy

questions.text: A laparoscopic hysterectomy is underway and the video monitor goes completely black. Which next steps are most appropriate to troubleshoot? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Apply antifog solution to the lens
B) Inspect and reconnect the light source
C) Irrigate the lens with warm saline
D) Inspect the camera cable and connections
E) Increase insufflation pressure

questions.explanation: A black screen points to light/camera failure—check the light source and camera connections. Fog/condensation degrade but don’t eliminate the image.

questions.text: Which lens angle provides the most straightforward and intuitive view during laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: A) 0-degree
B) 30-degree
C) 45-degree
D) 70-degree
E) 90-degree

questions.explanation: A 0° lens aligns with the natural line of sight, giving the most intuitive view.

questions.text: During operative hysteroscopy, which is essential to ensure full visibility of the operative instrument tip?

answerOptions.text: A) Use of flexible hysteroscope
B) Use of a 30-degree hysteroscope
C) Use of a 0-degree hysteroscope
D) Use of a small-diameter hysteroscope
E) Use of a 90-degree scope

questions.explanation: Only a 0° hysteroscope keeps the operative instrument tip fully in view.

questions.text: When using a 30-degree laparoscope, image orientation can be manipulated by:

answerOptions.text: A) Decreasing insufflation pressure
B) Moving the light cord opposite to the desired field of view
C) Rotating the scope 360 degrees rapidly
D) Changing to a zero-degree lens
E) Increasing camera zoom

questions.explanation: With angled scopes, rotating the light cord steers the viewing direction.

questions.text: Which of the following is an ignition source during laparoscopy?

answerOptions.text: A) Saline
B) Surgical drape
C) Betadine
D) Electrosurgery
E) Oxygen

questions.explanation: Electrosurgery is a heat/ignition source when fuel and oxidizer are present.

questions.text: Which scenario represents the lowest risk for a surgical fire?

answerOptions.text: A) Open oxygen + alcohol prep + cautery near the face
B) Alcohol prep with laparoscopic cautery
C) Electrosurgery below the xiphoid with betadine prep and general anesthesia
D) Cautery in the oral cavity under open oxygen
E) Laser on the scalp under nitrous oxide

questions.explanation: No open oxygen, no alcohol prep, and a lower anatomic site minimize fire risk.

questions.text: Capacitive coupling in electrosurgery refers to:

answerOptions.text: A) Intentional energy transfer to adjacent tissue via clamp
B) Electrode-to-metal arcing at the operative tip
C) Unintended energy transfer through intact insulation to nearby structures
D) Creation of a closed loop for monopolar circuit completion
E) Flow of current through the grounding pad

questions.explanation: Capacitive coupling transfers energy across intact insulation due to electric fields, causing unintended burns.

questions.text: First step when the laparoscopic image suddenly goes black during a procedure?

answerOptions.text: A) Clean the lens
B) Adjust insufflation pressure
C) Inspect the light source connection
D) Attach smoke evacuator
E) Increase camera zoom

questions.explanation: A black screen suggests loss of light/signal—check the light source first.

questions.text: All of the following can reduce visualization without making the screen go completely black EXCEPT:

answerOptions.text: A) Fogging of the laparoscope
B) Blood in the operative field
C) Camera disconnection
D) Smoke from cautery
E) Dirty lens

questions.explanation: Camera disconnection blacks out the image; other issues merely degrade it.

questions.text: To troubleshoot a blurry laparoscopic image caused by condensation, the most appropriate next step is:

answerOptions.text: A) Remove the camera and replace it
B) Soak the lens in betadine
C) Wipe the scope on a peritoneal surface
D) Increase insufflation pressure
E) Switch to a 0-degree scope

questions.explanation: A gentle wipe on a peritoneal surface removes condensation quickly.

questions.text: Which action will most directly improve visualization in a blood-filled uterine cavity during hysteroscopy?

answerOptions.text: A) Increase cervical dilation
B) Inject vasopressin
C) Increase fluid outflow with suction
D) Decrease intrauterine pressure
E) Use monopolar electrosurgery

questions.explanation: Suction/outflow clears blood and immediately improves the view.

questions.text: Which uterine phase offers optimal visualization for diagnostic hysteroscopy?

answerOptions.text: A) Luteal phase
B) Secretory phase
C) Early proliferative phase
D) Mid-cycle
E) Menstrual phase

questions.explanation: Early proliferative phase has a thin endometrium and minimal bleeding.

questions.text: What is the most important precaution before advancing a hysteroscope with poor visualization?

answerOptions.text: A) Reduce distension pressure
B) Place the patient in Trendelenburg
C) Use vasopressin in the cavity
D) Improve visualization before further advancement
E) Use a larger diameter hysteroscope

questions.explanation: Never advance blindly—optimize visualization first to avoid perforation.

questions.text: Which of the following can cause underdistension during hysteroscopy?

answerOptions.text: A) Small fibroids
B) Overdilation of the cervix
C) Use of vasopressin
D) Retroverted uterus
E) Use of saline

questions.explanation: Overdilation allows fluid egress, reducing cavity pressure and distension.
questions.submodule: Module 3.5 – Managing Poor Uterine Distension During Hysteroscopy

questions.text: A reasonable technique to manage underdistension caused by a wide cervical opening is:

answerOptions.text: A) Increase uterine pressure to 120 mmHg
B) Clamp the cervix with a sponge stick or Allis clamp
C) Decrease hysteroscope size to 2 mm
D) Apply uterine manipulator
E) Use vasopressin for hemostasis

questions.explanation: Clamping narrows the canal, reducing leakage and restoring pressure.
questions.submodule: Module 3.5 – Managing Poor Uterine Distension During Hysteroscopy

questions.text: Which best explains why overdilation leads to inadequate uterine distension?

answerOptions.text: A) Causes uterine rupture
B) Induces premature cervical ripening
C) Allows backflow of distension fluid
D) Increases intrauterine pressure beyond MAP
E) Decreases uterine contractility

questions.explanation: Overdilation compromises the seal, allowing fluid to escape.
questions.submodule: Module 3.5 – Managing Poor Uterine Distension During Hysteroscopy
